title: Hierarchical Operator Trees
subtitle: Symbolic + Geometric: organizing operators into trees where each node is BOTH a label AND an embedding region

children.children: This tutorial explores how Schankian operators can be organized into hierarchical trees that combine symbolic labels with geometric embedding regions. This is the architecture behind Paper 2c — handling polysemy, composition, and generalization in one unified structure.

props.title: 1. The Problem: Flat Lists Don't Scale

children.children: Schank originally proposed ~14 primitive operators (ATRANS, PTRANS, PROPEL, MOVE, GRASP, INGEST, EXPEL, MTRANS, MBUILD, SPEAK, ATTEND, SMELL, SEE, HEAR). But this flat list has problems:

items.definition: 'Shot him a glance' vs 'shot him dead' — the same surface verb maps to completely different operators. A flat list forces a hard choice. Which PROPEL is it?
Is 'stab' a kind of PROPEL? Is PROPEL a kind of CAUSE? Flat lists can't represent these IS-A relationships.
'Chop the onion' = SEPARATE ∘ CONTACT — but where does the composed operator live in a flat list?

left.title: Flat List (Schank 1975)
items: Like a filing cabinet with 14 folders

right.title: Hierarchical Tree (Our Approach)
items: Verbs map to REGIONS, not points

props.title: 2. The Key Insight: Nodes Are Dual

children.children: Here's the central idea: each node in the operator tree is BOTH a symbolic label AND a region in embedding space. Not one or the other — both simultaneously.

props.title: 🤔 Why both? Can't we just use embeddings?

children.children: Pure embeddings give you similarity and composition, but no human-interpretable labels. Pure symbols give you labels, but no way to handle graded membership or composition. The dual representation gives you:

1. INTERPRETABILITY — every region has a name you can inspect. 2. CONTINUITY — operators blend smoothly at boundaries. 3. COMPOSITIONALITY — geometric composition respects the symbolic tree structure.
The CAUSE node has:
**Symbolic side:** Label = 'CAUSE', children = [LET, HELP, HINDER, PREVENT] (from Talmy's force dynamics). Parent = ROOT.

**Geometric side:** A region in operator embedding space (think: a fuzzy ball). All verbs expressing causation land inside this region. 'Make', 'force', 'cause', 'compel' cluster near the center. 'Allow', 'let', 'permit' cluster in the LET sub-region.
**The dual constraint:** The geometric sub-regions MUST be nested inside the parent region. LET's embedding ball must be inside CAUSE's embedding ball. This is enforced during training.

props.title: 3. Tree Structure: From Abstract to Specific

children.children: The tree has roughly 3-4 levels of abstraction:

items.definition: The universal operator — 'something happened'. Encompasses all of embedding space.
CAUSE, TRANSFER, MOVE, PERCEIVE, THINK. Broad partitions of the space, roughly corresponding to Dowty's DO/CAUSE/BECOME/GO.
ATRANS, PTRANS, PROPEL, MBUILD, MTRANS, etc. Schank's original level — each is a sub-region of its parent.
CUT, STAB, THROW, KICK under PROPEL. GIVE, SELL, STEAL under ATRANS. Learned from data, not predefined.

children.children: The tree is NOT a rigid taxonomy! Regions overlap at boundaries. 'Teaching' involves both MTRANS (information transfer) and CAUSE (making someone learn). The geometric representation handles this gracefully — the verb 'teach' lands in the overlap region.
This is where hierarchical trees really shine. Consider the word 'shot':
**'He shot the target'** → CAUSE → PROPEL → SHOOT_PROJECTILE. The object 'target' pulls the embedding toward the physical-harm sub-region.
**'She shot him a glance'** → TRANSFER → MTRANS → COMMUNICATE_NONVERBAL. The object 'glance' pulls the embedding toward the information-transfer sub-region.
**'The director shot the scene'** → CAUSE → CREATE → RECORD. The subject 'director' + object 'scene' pull toward the creation sub-region.
Same surface verb, three completely different tree paths. The arguments (subject, object, context) act as gravitational pulls on the embedding, routing it through different branches of the tree.

props.title: 🔍 How does the routing actually work?

children.children: 1. Encode the verb + its arguments into an initial embedding vector. 2. At each tree level, compute similarity to each child node's region center. 3. Soft-route: assign probability weights to each child (not hard argmax). 4. The final operator is a weighted combination of leaf-level operators. This is differentiable end-to-end — the tree structure is learned, not hand-coded.

props.title: 🎮 Polysemy Router

children.children: The key training constraint: child regions must be geometrically nested inside parent regions. This is what makes the tree meaningful, not just decorative.

Euclidean space is a poor fit for trees — you need exponentially growing volume for each level. Hyperbolic space (Poincaré ball) naturally has this property: volume grows exponentially with radius. Tree roots near the center, leaves near the boundary.

**Poincaré Ball Model:** A unit disk where distances increase near the edge. The center = ROOT (most abstract). The boundary = maximally specific operators. Geodesics (shortest paths) are arcs, not straight lines. This gives us natural containment: a ball of radius r centered at a point contains exponentially more volume in hyperbolic space than Euclidean.

props.title: 🎮 Hyperbolic vs Euclidean Nesting

children.children: Complex events decompose into paths through the tree. 'John taught Mary calculus' isn't a single operator — it's a composition:
teach(John, Mary, calculus) = CAUSE(John, MTRANS(calculus, John → Mary)) ∘ MBUILD(Mary, calculus)

In tree terms: 1) CAUSE branch activates (John is causal agent). 2) MTRANS sub-branch activates (information transfers). 3) MBUILD sub-branch activates (Mary constructs knowledge). The composed operator lives in the INTERSECTION of these branches' geometric regions.

props.title: 🧮 How does geometric composition work in hyperbolic space?

children.children: Möbius addition in the Poincaré ball: x ⊕ y = ((1+2⟨x,y⟩+‖y‖²)x + (1-‖x‖²)y) / (1+2⟨x,y⟩+‖x‖²‖y‖²). This replaces vector addition for composing operators in hyperbolic space. The result respects the curvature — composed operators stay inside the ball and inherit the hierarchical structure.

props.title: 🎮 Operator Composition Tree

children.children: Metaphor is one of the hardest problems in NLP. Hierarchical operator trees give us an elegant account:
Literal meaning = full traversal to a leaf node. Metaphor = traversal that STOPS at an intermediate level or CROSSES between branches.

**'The news hit me hard'** — Literal: CAUSE → PROPEL → STRIKE (physical). Metaphorical: traverses CAUSE → PROPEL but then JUMPS to PERCEIVE → ATTEND → EMOTIONAL_IMPACT. The tree path starts in one branch but resolves in another.

**'She devoured the book'** — Literal: INGEST (eating). Metaphorical: CAUSE → INGEST-pattern but resolves to ATTEND → READ → INTENSE. The INGEST operator's geometric signature (rapid consumption, completeness) carries over even though the domain changed.

The higher up the tree the shared ancestor is, the MORE metaphorical the expression. 'Shot him dead' (shared at PROPEL level = literal). 'Shot him a glance' (shared only at ROOT level = highly metaphorical).

This explains why metaphor comprehension develops later in children — you need to have the full tree structure in place before you can do cross-branch mapping. Children first learn concrete operators (physical branches), then abstract ones (mental branches), then the cross-branch connections.
How do we actually learn this hierarchical structure? Three approaches, each with trade-offs:

items.definition: Start with all verbs in ROOT. Recursively split into sub-regions using spectral clustering on the operator embedding similarity matrix. Pro: guaranteed hierarchy. Con: splits are greedy and irreversible.
Start with individual verb operators as leaves. Merge the most similar pairs. Pro: fine-grained at bottom. Con: upper levels may be arbitrary.
Use a TreeVAE-like architecture where tree structure is learned jointly with embeddings. Pro: optimal for the task. Con: hard to train, potential mode collapse.

children.children: Use linguistic anchor points (Entry 9's KNN approach) for the top 2 levels — initialize Level 1 with Dowty's primitives (DO, CAUSE, BECOME, GO) and Level 2 with Schank's 14. Then let bottom-up agglomerative clustering discover Level 3 from data. Finally, fine-tune end-to-end with a containment loss that enforces nesting. Best of all three worlds.

props.title: 🎮 Containment Loss Calculator

children.children: The hierarchical operator tree connects to many other brainstorm entries:

items.definition: Provides the initialization strategy — Dowty primitives as anchor points for Level 1-2.
Refines the CAUSE branch: LET, HELP, HINDER, PREVENT as sub-nodes with distinct force-dynamic geometry.
Each tree level corresponds to factoring out one dimension: Level 1 = domain, Level 2 = transformation type, Level 3 = specific manner.
The tree IS the periodic table — organized by domain × transformation type, with gaps predicting missing operators.
MDL determines the optimal tree depth — too shallow = underfitting, too deep = overfitting.

children.children: Hierarchical operator trees solve three fundamental problems simultaneously:

1. POLYSEMY: Same verb → different tree paths based on context. No more forced disambiguation. 2. GRANULARITY: Operate at whatever level of abstraction the task requires. Coarse reasoning uses Level 1, detailed analysis uses Level 3. 3. GENERALIZATION: Novel verbs are placed in the tree by geometric similarity — even if never seen in training. If 'yeet' is geometrically near PROPEL/THROW, it inherits PROPEL's properties.

This is why it was PROMOTED TO PAPER 2c — it's the architectural backbone that makes everything else work. The tree provides the organizational principle; the geometric embeddings provide the computational mechanism; together they give us a system that's both interpretable (symbolic labels) and powerful (continuous representations).

questions.question: Why can't a flat list of Schankian primitives handle the word 'shot' in different contexts?
options: Because 'shot' isn't a real primitive
Because a flat list forces each verb to map to exactly one operator, but 'shot' needs different operators in different contexts
Because flat lists can't be embedded in vector space
Because Schank didn't include 'shot' in his original list
questions.explanation: A flat list provides no mechanism for context-dependent disambiguation. 'Shot him dead' (PROPEL) and 'shot him a glance' (MTRANS) need different operators, but a flat list offers only one slot per verb.

questions.question: What does it mean for a tree node to be 'dual' (symbolic + geometric)?
options: It has two parents in the tree
It stores both a human-readable label AND an embedding region in vector space
It can be used in two different languages
It has both a forward and inverse operator
questions.explanation: Each node carries a symbolic label (like 'CAUSE') for interpretability AND a geometric region in embedding space for continuous computation. This gives the best of both symbolic AI and neural approaches.

questions.question: Why is hyperbolic space better than Euclidean space for embedding operator trees?
options: Hyperbolic space is faster to compute
Euclidean space can't represent distances
Hyperbolic space has exponentially growing volume with radius, naturally fitting tree branching
Hyperbolic space has fewer dimensions
questions.explanation: Trees have exponentially many nodes at each level. Euclidean space grows polynomially (r³ in 3D), causing crowding at lower levels. Hyperbolic space grows exponentially — exactly matching tree structure. This is why Poincaré embeddings revolutionized hierarchy learning.

questions.question: How does the hierarchical tree account for metaphor?
options: Metaphors are stored as a separate flat list
The tree has a special 'metaphor' branch
Metaphor involves partial traversal or cross-branch mapping — the literal path starts in one branch but resolves in another
Metaphors can't be represented in the tree
questions.explanation: 'The news hit me hard' starts traversing the physical PROPEL branch but jumps to EMOTIONAL_IMPACT. The height of the shared ancestor measures how metaphorical an expression is — higher ancestor = more metaphorical.

questions.question: What does the containment loss enforce during training?
options: That all operators have the same size
That child embedding regions are geometrically nested inside parent regions
That the tree is perfectly balanced
That operators don't overlap
questions.explanation: Containment loss = max(0, dist(parent,child) + child_radius - parent_radius). It's zero when the child ball fits entirely inside the parent ball, and positive (penalizing) when the child sticks out. This enforces the tree structure geometrically.
